name: Lugo – tour around Castroverde and Castro de Viladonga
name_original: Lugo – vuelta por Castroverde y Castro de Viladonga
description: This difficult 46.5-mile touring cycling route around Lugo explores green landscapes and historical sites like Castro de Viladonga.
region_intro: Touring cycling around Lugo, Spain, offers diverse landscapes ranging from the scenic Miño River valley to significant mountain ranges like Os Ancares Lucenses and O Courel. The region features dramatic river canyons in Ribeira Sacra and a varied Cantabrian coastline. Cyclists can explore routes that combine natural beauty with historical sites, including segments of the Camino de Santiago. Best touring cycling routes around Lugo The most popular touring cycling route is Miño River and Lugo City Loop, a 9.2 miles (14.8 km) trail that takes 1 hour 3 minutes to complete. This route combines historical city sights with scenic riverside paths. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Outeiro de Rei – return via A Feira de Monte and Torre de Caldaloba, a moderate 28.7 miles (46.2 km) path. This route explores green landscapes and historical sites. Local touring cyclists also love the Portomarín and Sarria Loop, a 33.2 miles (53.5 km) trail leading through historical pilgrimage areas, often completed in about 3 hours 43 minutes. Touring cycling around Lugo is defined by the Miño River, mountain ranges, and the dramatic Ribeira Sacra region. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and difficult routes, catering to various ability levels. The routes in Lugo are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.6 stars from over 200 reviews. More than 1600 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Lugo's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available around Lugo?
A: There are over 140 touring cycling routes available around Lugo, catering to various skill levels. You'll find options ranging from easy riverside paths to challenging mountain ascents.

Q: What is the best time of year for touring cycling in Lugo?
A: Lugo offers pleasant touring cycling conditions for much of the year. Spring and autumn generally provide the most comfortable temperatures and beautiful scenery. Summers can be warm, especially inland, while winters are milder along the coast but can be wet.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on touring cycling routes in Lugo?
A: The terrain in Lugo is incredibly diverse. You can expect flat, scenic paths along the &lt;strong&gt;Miño River&lt;/strong&gt;, rolling hills through green landscapes, and more challenging climbs and descents in the mountainous regions like &lt;strong&gt;Os Ancares Lucenses&lt;/strong&gt; and &lt;strong&gt;O Courel&lt;/strong&gt;. The &lt;strong&gt;Ribeira Sacra&lt;/strong&gt; region features dramatic river canyons and terraced vineyards, offering unique and often demanding routes.

Q: Are there touring cycling routes suitable for beginners or families in Lugo?
A: Yes, Lugo has routes suitable for beginners and families. There are 11 easy routes available. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1017353176/mino-river-and-lugo-city-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Miño River and Lugo City Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 9.2-mile (14.8 km) trail that combines historical city sights with scenic riverside paths, offering a good option for a less strenuous ride.

Q: What natural features can I expect to see while cycling around Lugo?
A: You'll encounter a rich tapestry of natural beauty. The prominent &lt;strong&gt;Miño River&lt;/strong&gt; offers picturesque surroundings, while the province is home to significant mountain ranges like &lt;strong&gt;Os Ancares Lucenses&lt;/strong&gt; and &lt;strong&gt;O Courel&lt;/strong&gt;, part of UNESCO Biosphere Reserves. The dramatic river canyons of &lt;strong&gt;Ribeira Sacra&lt;/strong&gt; and the charming &lt;strong&gt;Cantabrian coastline&lt;/strong&gt; to the north also provide stunning natural backdrops.

Q: Are there historical landmarks or cultural sites along the touring cycling routes in Lugo?
A: Absolutely. Lugo is rich in history. Many routes integrate natural scenery with historical sites. You can cycle past the ancient &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/334304" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Roman Wall of Lugo&lt;/a&gt;, a UNESCO World Heritage site, or explore segments of the &lt;strong&gt;Camino de Santiago&lt;/strong&gt;. Other historical elements include Roman baths, medieval structures, and ancient forts. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1018468989/outeiro-de-rei-return-via-a-feira-de-monte-and-torre-de-caldaloba" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Outeiro de Rei – return via A Feira de Monte and Torre de Caldaloba&lt;/a&gt; route, for instance, explores green landscapes and historical sites.

Q: Can I find routes that offer great viewpoints in the Lugo region?
A: Yes, the mountainous areas of Lugo, particularly &lt;strong&gt;Os Ancares Lucenses&lt;/strong&gt; and &lt;strong&gt;O Courel&lt;/strong&gt;, offer high viewpoints with stunning vistas. The terraced vineyards of &lt;strong&gt;Ribeira Sacra&lt;/strong&gt; also provide spectacular panoramic views over the river canyons. Many routes are designed to showcase these breathtaking landscapes.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes around Lugo?
A: Yes, many touring cycling routes in Lugo are designed as loops, allowing you to start and end in the same location. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1017348492/portomarin-and-sarria-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Portomarín and Sarria Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a 33.2-mile (53.5 km) trail that takes you through historical pilgrimage areas.

Q: Are there touring cycling routes that pass by cafes or pubs for a break?
A: Many routes, especially those passing through towns or villages, will offer opportunities to stop at local cafes, pubs, or restaurants. The region's charming rural villages and historical towns are excellent places to refuel and experience local Galician hospitality. Routes along the Camino de Santiago, in particular, are well-equipped with amenities for travelers.

Q: What about parking facilities for touring cyclists in Lugo?
A: Parking availability varies by location. In larger towns like Lugo city, you'll find designated parking areas, some of which may be paid. For routes starting in smaller villages or rural areas, roadside parking or small public car parks are often available. It's advisable to check specific starting points for parking information.

Q: Is public transport accessible for reaching touring cycling routes in Lugo?
A: Public transport options, primarily buses, connect many towns and villages in Lugo province. Some routes, particularly those along the Camino de Santiago, are well-served. However, carrying bicycles on public transport can sometimes be restricted or require special arrangements, so it's best to check with local transport providers in advance.

Q: What do other touring cyclists enjoy the most about touring cycling in Lugo?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.6 stars from over 200 reviews. Reviewers often praise the diverse landscapes, from the tranquil river valleys to the challenging mountain passes, and the seamless blend of natural beauty with historical and cultural sites, including segments of the Camino de Santiago.
tips: Defensive tower still preserved of the Roman Wall of Lugo, which surrounds the historic center of the city.

If you are walking the Camino Primitivo, arriving here means that you are one step away from entering the city of Lugo. A sign tells you that you only have 100 kilometers left to get to Santiago de Compostela, but first you have to enjoy the hospitality, gastronomy and monuments of this beautiful city.
Small church of medieval origin (Romanesque door) with later transformations. Masonry construction of a single nave, roofed on three sides with slate. Small rectangular apse.

The Church of Santiago de Espasande is one of the prominent monuments in the area. Popularly known as the “Castroverde Cathedral” for its monumentality, it was declared a National Monument in 1979. Built in 1557 by Fernando de Castro and renovated by Diego Osorio in the 17th century, it presents a late ogival style with Gothic-Romanesque influences. 😊

This 15th-century church is mostly Gothic in style. However, due to the renovations and restorations that took place in later centuries, the current appearance has baroque and neoclassical elements. At the end of the 20th century it was declared an asset of cultural interest.
The Castro de Viladonga, is a defensive fortress of archaeological importance from the Galician-Roman era. The site has a free museum and cultural center.

faqs.question: How challenging is the Lugo – tour around Castroverde and Castro de Viladonga cycling route?
faqs.answer: This is classified as a difficult cycling route, requiring very good fitness. It spans approximately 74.8 kilometers (46.5 miles) with an elevation gain of around 1,080 meters (3,543 feet). While some parts have a soft unevenness, other segments can be demanding due to length and elevation.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this cycling tour?
faqs.answer: The route features a mix of terrain. Much of it is on paved surfaces, but be prepared for some unpaved segments that might be challenging and could require you to push your bike. You'll cycle through lush green landscapes, gentle hills, and some more demanding sections typical of the Lugo province.

faqs.question: What are the main scenic and historical highlights along the route?
faqs.answer: The tour combines natural beauty with significant historical sites. You'll encounter the ancient Celtic settlement of Castro de Viladonga, the ruins of Castroverde's 14th-century castle, and the Gothic-style Church of Saint Mary of Vilabade. Other notable points include Ronda da Muralla and various religious buildings like the Church of Saint Tomé and the Church of Santiago de Espasande.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The region is known for its green landscapes, suggesting pleasant conditions during spring and early autumn. These seasons typically offer milder temperatures and less extreme weather, making the long distance and elevation gain more enjoyable. Summer can also be good, but be mindful of potential heat.

faqs.question: Where can I park my car to start this cycling tour?
faqs.answer: Given the tour's start and end points near Lugo, you can look for parking options in Lugo itself, particularly near the Ronda da Muralla. If you plan to start closer to Castroverde or Castro de Viladonga, check for local parking facilities in those villages, often near their main attractions or town centers.

faqs.question: Can I access the starting point of the tour using public transport?
faqs.answer: Lugo, as a provincial capital, is well-connected by public transport, including bus and train services. You can likely reach Lugo by public transport and begin your cycling tour from there. For Castroverde or Castro de Viladonga, local bus services might be available, but their frequency could be limited, especially on weekends.

faqs.question: Is the Lugo – tour around Castroverde and Castro de Viladonga route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While cycling routes themselves are generally open, the suitability for dogs depends on their fitness level for such a long and difficult ride, and whether they can keep up safely. If your dog is in a trailer or basket, ensure it's secure and comfortable for the duration. Always check local regulations for specific sites or natural areas you might pass through, and keep your dog on a leash, especially near roads or populated areas.

faqs.question: Do I need any special permits or is there an entrance fee for this cycling tour?
faqs.answer: Generally, no special permits or entrance fees are required to cycle this route. However, the tour passes through the Reserva da Biosfera Terras do Miño. While cycling through a biosphere reserve is typically permitted, always respect local regulations regarding protected areas, waste disposal, and wildlife. Entrance to specific historical sites like the museum at Castro de Viladonga may have a separate fee.

faqs.question: Does this cycling route intersect with other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route intersects with several other paths. Notably, it covers a significant portion of the Etapa 9: O Cádavo - Lugo (24% of the route) and also touches upon the historic Vía Romana XIX (2%). You'll also find smaller sections overlapping with O Camiño Primitivo, part of the Camino de Santiago network, particularly around Castroverde.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or find accommodation along the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, as the route passes through villages like Castroverde and ends in Lugo, you will find options for cafes, restaurants, and accommodation. Castroverde, being on the Primitive Way of the Camino de Santiago, has historical ties to pilgrims and offers services. Lugo, as a larger city, provides a wide range of amenities. It's advisable to check opening hours, especially in smaller villages, and consider booking accommodation in advance.

waypoints.name: Church of Saint Mary of Vilabade
